meta.name: Pulled Pork (Smoked Pork Shoulder, Classic BBQ)

source.name: SSA Canonical Catalog
notes: Classic low-and-slow pork shoulder for pulled pork, written for SSA planners/adapters.
Includes bark-building, wrap options, doneness cues, and safe cooling/storage guidance.

yield.notes: A 8–10 lb bone-in shoulder typically yields ~50–60% cooked meat by weight. Plan 1/3–1/2 lb cooked meat per adult serving.

ingredients.label: Pork shoulder / Boston butt (bone-in preferred)
ingredients.notes: 8–10 lb ideal. Bone-in helps moisture; boneless works too.

ingredients.label: Yellow mustard (binder, optional)
ingredients.notes: Helps rub adhere; flavor cooks off.

ingredients.label: Kosher salt
ingredients.notes: If using table salt, reduce amount. Salt is key for bark and seasoning.

ingredients.label: Black pepper (coarse ground)
ingredients.notes: Texas-style simple rub is often just salt + pepper; this recipe adds classic BBQ spices too.

ingredients.label: Brown sugar
ingredients.notes: Optional for sweetness. Reduce/omit if you prefer less sweet bark or are cooking hotter.

ingredients.label: Apple juice (spritz / wrap moisture)
ingredients.notes: Use for spritzing or add a splash when wrapping. Can substitute apple cider vinegar + water.

ingredients.label: Apple cider vinegar (spritz option)
ingredients.notes: Mix 1:1 with water for spritz; balances sweetness and keeps surface tacky.

ingredients.label: BBQ sauce (serving, optional)
ingredients.notes: Serve on the side or mix lightly into pulled pork with drippings.

ingredients.label: Vinegar finishing splash (optional)
ingredients.notes: A small splash brightens pulled pork and balances richness.

ingredients.label: Smoking wood (oak/hickory/pecan/apple/cherry)
ingredients.notes: Amount depends on smoker type. Hickory/pecan are classic; fruit woods add sweetness.

ingredients.label: Buns (for sandwiches, optional)
ingredients.notes: Brioche, potato rolls, or standard hamburger buns.

ingredients.label: Cole slaw (for serving, optional)
ingredients.notes: Classic pulled pork pairing; serve on top of sandwiches.

steps.title: Trim and prep the pork shoulder
steps.body: Pat pork shoulder dry. Trim only thick, hard exterior fat caps down to about 1/4 inch—leave most fat and collagen for moisture. Score fat cap lightly (optional) to help render. Let meat sit at room temperature 20–30 minutes while smoker preheats.

steps.title: Mix the dry rub
steps.body: Combine kosher salt, black pepper, paprika, garlic powder, onion powder, chili powder, and optional brown sugar/cayenne. Mix thoroughly and break up any brown sugar clumps.

steps.title: Season pork shoulder
steps.body: Optional: apply a thin coat of yellow mustard as binder. Apply rub generously on all sides, pressing to adhere. For best bark, rest uncovered in the fridge 2–12 hours; otherwise rest at room temp 20–30 minutes while smoker stabilizes.

steps.title: Preheat smoker
steps.body: Preheat smoker to 225–250°F (107–121°C). Add smoking wood and aim for clean smoke (thin blue). If using a water pan, fill it for temperature stability and humidity.

steps.title: Smoke to build bark (unwrapped)
steps.body: Place pork shoulder on smoker, fat cap oriented based on your heat source (fat toward heat can help protect). Smoke at 225–250°F until bark is dark and set and internal temp is typically 160–170°F. This commonly takes 5–7 hours depending on size and cooker. Optional: spritz lightly every 60–90 minutes after the first 2 hours once bark is set.

steps.title: Wrap through the stall (optional but common)
steps.body: When bark is set and internal temp is ~160–170°F, you may wrap to push through the stall and preserve moisture. Wrap tightly in foil or butcher paper. Add a small splash of apple juice if using foil. Return to smoker and continue cooking at 250°F until probe-tender.

steps.title: Cook until probe-tender (doneness by feel)
steps.body: Continue cooking until the thickest part is probe-tender—an instant-read probe slides in with little resistance, like warm butter. Internal temp is often 195–205°F, but texture matters more than a single number. Check multiple spots; the shoulder should feel soft and yielding.

steps.title: Rest (critical for juicy pulled pork)
steps.body: Rest wrapped pork at least 45–60 minutes (up to 2 hours) in a dry cooler or warm oven (around 150–170°F) to redistribute juices and soften collagen. Do not skip this step for best moisture.

steps.title: Pull the pork and season to taste
steps.body: Unwrap and reserve juices/drippings. Remove bone (it should slide out clean). Shred meat with gloved hands or forks, discarding large fat chunks if desired. Mix in a few tablespoons of reserved drippings for moisture. Optional: add a small splash of vinegar to brighten and/or a light amount of BBQ sauce. Taste and adjust salt/pepper.

steps.title: Serve
steps.body: Serve as sandwiches with buns and slaw, or as a BBQ plate with beans, potato salad, cornbread, or roasted vegetables. Keep warm in a covered pan; moisten with a little drippings as needed.

steps.title: Storage and reheating
steps.body: Cool quickly for food safety: spread meat in a shallow pan to drop temperature, then refrigerate within 2 hours. Refrigerate up to 4 days or freeze up to 3 months. Reheat gently covered with a splash of drippings/water (275°F oven) until hot, or warm on stovetop over low heat.

leftovers.notes: Plan as a weekend batch cook. Freeze in 1–2 lb portions with a little drippings for best reheating.

serveModes.label: Sandwiches + Slaw
serveModes.notes: Best for crowds; keeps well warm.

notes: No smoker: roast in oven at 275°F covered until tender (6–8 hours), then uncover briefly to dry the surface; broil lightly for bark-like edges if desired.
Pellet grill: stable temp is easy; spritz less often to avoid washing off bark.
Probe tenderness is the main indicator. If it’s tough at 195°F, keep cooking and re-check.
If meat shreds dry, it likely needed more rest and/or a bit of drippings mixed back in.
For Carolina-style, skip heavy sauce and finish with a vinegar-pepper splash.
For sweeter profile, keep brown sugar in rub and set with a light sauce after pulling.
